coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient, targeted, and traceable perovskite nanocrystals for photoelectrocatalytic oncotherapy

coredata.dc:description: Metal halide perovskites have emerged as highly promising materials in optoelectronics, owing to their unique multidimensional crystal structures that impart exceptional optical and electronic properties. These materials exhibit remarkable fluorescence imaging and tracking capabilities, as well as efficient photoelectric conversion, making them suitable for a broad range of applications. Nevertheless, despite their significant potential, their poor water stability has posed a major challenge, particularly in biomedical fields such as drug delivery systems, biological imaging, and photoelectrocatalytic oncotherapy. This limitation has hindered their practical use in medical treatments and diagnostics. In this study, we address the water stability issue by successfully synthesizing CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 perovskite nanocrystals (PeNCs) and conjugating them with methotrexate-chitosan-folic acid (MTX-CS-FA), resulting in innovative green light-emitting PeNCs@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles. These nanoparticles exhibited remarkable water stability, maintaining their structural and functional integrity for up to 228 days, a significant improvement that enables their application in complex biological environments. Under visible light illumination, the nanoparticles demonstrated a dual-action therapeutic mechanism. The perovskites effectively generated electrons and reactive oxygen species (ROS), inducing oxidative stress in tumor cells. At the same time, photogenerated holes oxidized glutathione (GSH), a molecule that is typically overexpressed in tumor cells to protect against oxidative damage. By depleting GSH, the nanoparticles weakened the tumor cells' defense mechanisms, thereby enhancing the oxidative damage caused by ROS. In addition, methotrexate (MTX), a chemotherapeutic agent integrated into the system, inhibited dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) activity. This inhibition disrupted tumor cell metabolism, particularly nucleotide synthesis, leading to lipid peroxidation and subsequent cell death. Together, these mechanisms generated a potent, synergistic therapeutic effect. The therapeutic efficacy of the PeNCs@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles was validated through in vivo antitumor experiments in mice. A total dose of 2.4 ​mg of nanoparticles resulted in a 63.68 ​% reduction in tumor volume and a 63.26 ​% decrease in tumor weight, demonstrating significant tumor growth suppression. Biological safety evaluations further confirmed the nanoparticles' biocompatibility. Notably, they were excreted from the mice in their fluorescent form without decomposition, ensuring minimal long-term toxicity. This safe excretion pathway underscores the feasibility of repeated use of these nanoparticles in clinical applications. Overall, this study highlights the transformative potential of metal halide perovskites in cancer treatment. By overcoming the water stability limitations that have previously constrained their biomedical applications, the PeNCs@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles exhibited outstanding capabilities in real-time bioimaging and effective photoelectrocatalytic chemotherapy, thus paving the way for future innovations in biomedical science.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The mechanisms by which CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles inhibit tumour cell growth, including photoelectrocatalytic therapy and chemotherapy. Schematic diagram of tumour-targeted CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles that produce ROS and release MTX in an alkaline environment to precisely kill tumour cells under visible light irradiation.

(a) XRD spectra of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA (red) and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 (grey). (b) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA (red) and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 (grey). (c) TEM, (d) HRTEM, and fast Fourier transform images (shown in the upper right corner) and (e) selected electron diffraction image of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3. (f) TEM, (g) HRTEM, and fast Fourier transform images (shown in the upper right corner) and (h) selected electron diffraction image of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA. (i) N 1s, (j) Pb 4f, and (k) Sn 3 ​d XPS spectra of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA (red), CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA in the simulated tumour environment (blue), and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 (grey).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal halide perovskites have emerged as highly promising materials in optoelectronics, owing to their unique multidimensional crystal structures that impart exceptional optical and electronic properties. These materials exhibit remarkable fluorescence imaging and tracking capabilities, as well as efficient photoelectric conversion, making them suitable for a broad range of applications. Nevertheless, despite their significant potential, their poor water stability has posed a major challenge, particularly in biomedical fields such as drug delivery systems, biological imaging, and photoelectrocatalytic oncotherapy. This limitation has hindered their practical use in medical treatments and diagnostics. In this study, we address the water stability issue by successfully synthesizing CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 perovskite nanocrystals (PeNCs) and conjugating them with methotrexate-chitosan-folic acid (MTX-CS-FA), resulting in innovative green light-emitting PeNCs@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles. These nanoparticles exhibited remarkable water stability, maintaining their structural and functional integrity for up to 228 days, a significant improvement that enables their application in complex biological environments. Under visible light illumination, the nanoparticles demonstrated a dual-action therapeutic mechanism. The perovskites effectively generated electrons and reactive oxygen species (ROS), inducing oxidative stress in tumor cells. At the same time, photogenerated holes oxidized glutathione (GSH), a molecule that is typically overexpressed in tumor cells to protect against oxidative damage. By depleting GSH, the nanoparticles weakened the tumor cells' defense mechanisms, thereby enhancing the oxidative damage caused by ROS. In addition, methotrexate (MTX), a chemotherapeutic agent integrated into the system, inhibited dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) activity. This inhibition disrupted tumor cell metabolism, particularly nucleotide synthesis, leading to lipid peroxidation and subsequent cell death. Together, these mechanisms generated a potent, synergistic therapeutic effect. The therapeutic efficacy of the PeNCs@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles was validated through in vivo antitumor experiments in mice. A total dose of 2.4 ​mg of nanoparticles resulted in a 63.68 ​% reduction in tumor volume and a 63.26 ​% decrease in tumor weight, demonstrating significant tumor growth suppression. Biological safety evaluations further confirmed the nanoparticles' biocompatibility. Notably, they were excreted from the mice in their fluorescent form without decomposition, ensuring minimal long-term toxicity. This safe excretion pathway underscores the feasibility of repeated use of these nanoparticles in clinical applications. Overall, this study highlights the transformative potential of metal halide perovskites in cancer treatment. By overcoming the water stability limitations that have previously constrained their biomedical applications, the PeNCs@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles exhibited outstanding capabilities in real-time bioimaging and effective photoelectrocatalytic chemotherapy, thus paving the way for future innovations in biomedical science.

The mechanisms by which CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles inhibit tumour cell growth, including photocatalytic therapy and chemotherapy. ce:section.ce:para: The aim of photoelectrocatalysis is to convert light energy into chemical energy. Photoelectrocatalysis has been employed not only in energy catalysis [1–4] but also in disease treatment by harnessing the abundant reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by photoinduced electrons [5,6] and the depletion of glutathione (GSH) through oxidation by photogenerated holes [7] Perovskite materials with excellent photoelectric conversion properties have garnered particular interest in the photovoltaic field [8–12]. These materials have the typical chemical formula of ABX3, with A representing a cation, B representing a bivalent cation, and X representing a halogen ion [13]. Perovskite materials exhibit high electron (2000 ​cm2 ​V−1 ​s−1) and hole (300 ​cm2 ​V−1 ​s−1) mobilities [14], as well as a long carrier diffusion distance of approximately 175 ​μm [15,16], which are key factors in photoelectrocatalytic performance. In biological imaging and targeted tracking, all-inorganic cesium lead halide perovskites with a high photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY > 80 ​%) [17,18] show great promise [19–21]. Grätzel et al. [22–24] conducted extensive studies on the use of perovskite in solar cells and photocatalysis. Yang et al. [25,26] delved deeply into the use of perovskite materials in the field of photocatalysis. Additionally, Ryu et al. [27], Lian et al. [28], and Patel et al. [29] utilized perovskite materials (CsPbBr3@SiO2) for cell imaging and demonstrated their significant intracellular fluorescence imaging properties. Under visible light illumination, perovskite nanoparticles have been reported to transfer energy from the triplet state to organic molecules, generating a significant number of electrons and holes that mediate the production of singlet oxygen (1O2) [30–33]. This excellent photoelectrocatalytic ability holds potential for antitumor therapy. However, the crystal structure of perovskite is susceptible to degradation by water [34–37]. Moisture can induce water-assisted decomposition [38–40], leading to the deterioration of photoelectric properties, the release of lead ions, and ultimately, cellular injury [41]. Compared with photoelectrical materials such as InP [42], AgInS [43], Carbon [44], CdSe [45], and ZnSe [46], perovskite demonstrates better overall performances, including photoelectrical properties, stability, and processability [47]. Designing perovskite nanocrystals (PeNCs) for biomedical applications presents significant challenges; for example, perovskite materials suitable for antitumor therapy must exhibit high water stability and low toxicity [48,49]. Methods to enhance the water resistance of metal halide PeNCs include coating with metal-organic frameworks [50], transition metal oxide glasses [51], and polymers [52].

In this study, chitosan (CS) is employed to modify CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 perovskite nanoparticles for enhancing their hydrophilicity. Moreover, folic acid (FA) and the chemotherapeutic drug methotrexate (MTX) are conjugated to CS via amide bonds. CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles with water stability, biological imaging, and anti-tumor capabilities are synthesized, and their mechanisms of action are elucidated.

Cs2CO3 (99 ​%), PbBr2 (99 ​%), 1-octadecene (OCE) ​> ​90 ​%, cyclohexane (C6H12) 99.5 ​%), oleic acid (OA) ≥99 ​%, oleylamine (OAm) (80–90 ​%), folic acid >97 ​%, N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) (98 ​%), glacial acetic acid ≥99.8%, H2O2 (30 ​wt % in H2O), 1-Ethyl-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) (98%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) ≥99 ​%, 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-4-piperidone hydrochloride (TEMP) (98%), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) (97 ​%), xylene (99 ​%), neutral gum, and ethanol (99.5 ​%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. SnBr2 (99.99 ​%), methyl acetate (98 ​%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Chitosan (CS) (M.W. 100,000–300,000) was purchased from Beijing Bailingwei Technology Co., Ltd. Methotrexate was purchased from Guangdong Lingnan Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) biological reagents were produced in Uruguay, South America. DMEM incomplete medium biological reagents, anti-fluorescence attenuation mounting medium (with DAPI) biological reagents, Phosphate-Buffered Saline (PBS) biological reagents, and trypsin biological reagent were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. CCK-8 kit biological reagents and Calcein-AM/PI live/dead cells double-staining kit biological reagents were purchased from Shijiazhuang Zhibao Biotechnology Co., Ltd. 4 ​% paraformaldehyde was purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. Lyso-Tracker Red (Lysosome Red Fluorescent Probe) biological reagent, Dihydroethidium (superoxide anion fluorescent probe) biological reagent, one-step TdT-mediated dUTP nick end labeling (TUNEL) cell apoptosis detection kit (red fluorescence) biological reagent, hematoxylin-eosin staining biological reagents, and RIPA Lysis Buffer (Strong) were supplied by Beyotime Biotech Inc., Shanghai. C57BL/6JNifdc clean-grade mice were supplied by Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. 5 ​% Chloral Hydrate was purchased from Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Tetrahydrofolate (THFA) detection kit biological reagent was purchased from Cloud-Clone Corp. (CCC, Wuhan). Anti-Glutathione antibody biological reagent was purchased from the British company Abcam. R-PE-conjugated AffiniPure F(ab')2 Fragment Donkey Anti-Mouse IgG (H ​+ ​L) biological reagent was purchased from Wuhan Sanying Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Dialysis bags (MW: 3500) were purchased from Nantong Supin Experimental Equipment Co., Ltd. BD Biosciences perm/wash buffer (BD perm/wash), BD Cytofix/Cytoperm™ Cell Fixation/Permeabilization Kit biological reagents were supplied by BD Biosciences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA, and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles
Synthesis of three-dimensional perovskite CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 nanocrystals (NCs) via hot injection method
ce:section.ce:para:
The CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 nanocrystals (NCs) are prepared using a hot injection method, consisting of the following steps:
(1)
Preparation of precursor solution

Dissolve 0.5 ​mmol PbBr2 and 0.5 ​mmol SnBr2 in 0.5 ​mL 1-octadecene (ODE) while stirring at 120 ​°C until PbBr2 and SnBr2 are completely dissolved. This forms the precursor solution. Then, add 1 ​mL oleic acid (OA) and 1.5 ​mL oleylamine (OAm) while stirring.
(2)
Preparation of cesium oleate (Cs-OL)

Dissolve 0.16 ​g Cs2CO3 in 1 ​mL oleic acid and 16 ​mL 1-octadecene, and stir at 120 ​°C until Cs2CO3 is completely dissolved. This results in the formation of cesium oleate (Cs-OL).
(3)
Injection of cesium oleate

Inject the cesium oleate solution into the precursor solution. After 5 ​min, the solution undergoes a reaction and transforms into a turbid white suspension.
(4)
Centrifugation and precipitation

Cool the white suspension to room temperature. Then, centrifuge the solution at 6000 r∙min−1 for 15 ​min to collect the white precipitate.
(5)
Ultrasonication and dispersion

Add 20 ​mL cyclohexane to the white precipitate and sonicate the mixture for 30 ​min. After uniform dispersion, zero-dimensional Cs4 Sn0.5Pb0.5Br6 nanocrystals (NCs) are obtained. This solution is stored at 4 ​°C for future use.
(6)
Preparation of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 NCs

Add 1 ​mL deionized water to 5 ​mL of the NCs-cyclohexane solution. Stir until the solution turns green. Once the color stabilizes, sonicate the mixed solution in the vial for 2–10 ​min. Finally, filter the solution using a 0.22 ​μm filter to obtain the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 NCs solution.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Prepare a solution of Cs4Sn0.5Pb0.5Br6 NCs as described in Section 2.2.1.
Dissolve chitosan in 1 ​% glacial acetic acid to prepare a chitosan solution with a concentration of 0.001 ​g/mL.

Stir the Cs4Sn0.5Pb0.5Br6 NCs solution and slowly add the chitosan solution into Cs4Sn0.5Pb0.5Br6 NCs solution in a volume ratio of 5 : 1. The white solution will gradually turn green. Once the color stabilizes, subject the mixed solution to ultrasonication for 2–10 ​min, followed by filtration using a 0.22 ​μm filter. The filtrate obtained is the chitosan-coated CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 nanoparticle solution, hereafter referred to as CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS nanoparticles. The CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS nanoparticles are extracted using methyl acetate, in a 1 : 2 ​vol ratio of methyl acetate to the filtrate. The extracted solution is then centrifuged at 6000 r∙min−1 for 15 ​min, and the precipitate is dried in a vacuum oven to obtain CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS nanoparticle powder.

Dissolve folic acid (FA) in DMSO to prepare a FA solution with a concentration of 1 ​mg/mL. Then, add C8H18ClN3 (EDC) at a concentration of 0.066 ​g/mL and C4H5NO3 (NHS) at a concentration of 0.036 ​g/mL to the solution. Stir the mixture at room temperature until the EDC and NHS are completely dissolved to obtain the FA solution.
Dissolve chitosan (CS) in 1 ​% glacial acetic acid to prepare a CS solution with a concentration of 0.001 ​g/mL.
Mix the FA solution and CS solution in a 1 : 5 ​vol ratio, followed by a reaction at room temperature, resulting in the formation of the CS-FA solution.

Add the CS-FA solution to Cs4Sn0.5Pb0.5Br6 solution at a 1 : 5 ​vol ratio, leading to a gradual color change from white to green. Once the color of the solution stabilizes, sonicate the mixture for 2–10 ​min, followed by filtration using a 0.22 ​μm filter. The filtrate is the chitosan-coated CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticle solution. Extraction is performed using ethyl acetate, with a 1 : 2 ​vol ratio of ethyl acetate to filtrate. The extracted solution is centrifuged at 6000 r∙min−1 for 15 ​min, and the precipitate is dried in a vacuum oven to obtain CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticle powder.
Prepare the CS-FA solution as described in section 2.2.3.

Dissolve methotrexate (MTX) in DMSO to obtain a solution with a concentration of 0.001 ​g/mL. Then, add C8H18ClN3 (EDC) at a concentration of 0.013 ​g/mL and C4H5NO3 (NHS) at a concentration of 0.007 ​g/mL to the solution. Stir the mixture at room temperature to obtain the MTX solution.
Add the CS-FA solution to the MTX solution at a 1 : 1 ​vol ratio at room temperature to obtain the MTX-CS-FA solution.

Add the MTX-CS-FA solution to the Cs4Sn0.5Pb0.5Br6 solution at a 1 : 1 ​vol ratio, resulting in a gradual color transformation from transparent to green. Once the color of the solution stabilizes, sonicate the mixed solution for 2–10 ​min, followed by filtration using a 0.22 ​μm filter. The resulting solution is CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles coated with CS. Extraction is performed using ethyl acetate at a 1 : 2 ​vol ratio of ethyl acetate to filtrate. The extracted solution is centrifuged at 6000 r∙min−1 for 15 ​min, and the precipitate is dried in a vacuum oven to obtain CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle powder.

ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles were obtained using an FEI Talos F200S microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis of CS, FA, MTX, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles was performed using a UV/vis 1050 FTIR instrument. XPS and XRD analyses of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles were conducted using a Shimadzu AXI Sultra DLD and a D8 ADVANCE instrument. The characterization of the UV photoelectron spectrum, UV–visible absorption spectrum, and fluorescence spectrum of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA, and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles was performed using a Thermo Fisher Escalab Xi+, a UV/vis 1050, and an FS5 spectrometer. Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) tests were conducted using a self-developed multifunctional atomic force microscope equipped with an NT-MDT SMENA head. The tests were carried out using an HA-FM/Pt conductive probe, and the results were analyzed and plotted using the open-source software Gwyddion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell confocal imaging experiment of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA, and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Cell confocal imaging experiments were conducted to illustrate the targeting of nanoparticles to tumor cells using a confocal microscope (FV1000, Olympus, Japan) [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell confocal imaging experiment of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS nanoparticles

ce:list-item.ce:para: Position cell coverslips at the bottom of each well in a 24-well plate and inoculate LLC cells at a density of approximately 2 ​× ​104 ​cells per well.
Culture the plate in a cell culture incubator for 24 ​h.
Remove the culture medium and add the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS solution with a concentration of 200 ​μg/mL, followed by an additional 6-h incubation.
Discard the culture medium and wash the cells three times with PBS to remove any unabsorbed quantum dots.
Add Lyso-Tracker Red at a concentration of 75 ​nmol ​L−1 to the cell culture medium and incubate at 37 ​°C for 45 ​min.
Wash the cells three times with PBS.
Fix the cells by adding 4 ​% paraformaldehyde for 30 ​min, followed by three washes with PBS.
Remove the cell coverslips and place them (cell side facing downwards) onto glass slides containing a mounting medium with DAPI.
Allow the slides to air-dry in the dark.

Observe and capture images using a confocal microscope (FV1000, Olympus, Japan). The cell nuclei are stained with DAPI and emit blue fluorescence upon excitation by a 405 ​nm laser. CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS nanoparticles exhibit green fluorescence when excited by a 488 ​nm laser. Lysosomes stained with Lyso-Tracker Red display red fluorescence upon excitation by a 559 ​nm laser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell confocal imaging experiment of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticles

ce:list-item.ce:para: Position cell coverslips at the bottom of each well in a 24-well plate and inoculate LLC and C2C12 ​cells at a density of approximately 2 ​× ​104 ​cells per well.
Culture the plate in a cell culture incubator for 24 ​h.
Remove the culture medium and add the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA solution with a concentration of 200 ​μg/mL, followed by an additional 6-h incubation.
Discard the culture medium and wash the cells three times with PBS to remove any unabsorbed quantum dots.
Add Lyso-Tracker Red at a concentration of 75 ​nmol ​L−1 to the cell culture medium and incubate at 37 ​°C for 45 ​min.
Wash the cells three times with PBS.
Fix the cells by adding 4 ​% paraformaldehyde for 30 ​min, followed by three washes with PBS.
Remove the cell coverslips and place them (cell side facing downwards) onto glass slides containing a mounting medium with DAPI.
Allow the slides to air-dry in the dark.

Observe and capture images using a confocal microscope (FV1000, Olympus, Japan). The cell nuclei are stained with DAPI and emit blue fluorescence upon excitation by a 405 ​nm laser. CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS nanoparticles exhibit green fluorescence when excited by a 488 ​nm laser. Lysosomes stained with Lyso-Tracker Red display red fluorescence upon excitation by a 559 ​nm laser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell confocal imaging experiment of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles

ce:list-item.ce:para: Position cell coverslips at the bottom of each well in a 24-well plate and inoculate LLC cells at a density of approximately 2 ​× ​104 ​cells per well.
Culture the plate in a cell culture incubator for 24 ​h.
Remove the culture medium and add the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA solution with a concentration of 200 ​μg/mL, followed by an additional 6-h incubation.
Discard the culture medium and wash the cells three times with PBS to remove any unabsorbed quantum dots.
Add Lyso-Tracker Red at a concentration of 75 ​nmol ​L−1 to the cell culture medium and incubate at 37 ​°C for 45 ​min.
Wash the cells three times with PBS.
Fix the cells by adding 4 ​% paraformaldehyde for 30 ​min, followed by three washes with PBS.
Remove the cell coverslips and place them (cell side facing downwards) onto glass slides containing a mounting medium with DAPI.
Allow the slides to air-dry in the dark.

Observe and capture images using a confocal microscope (FV1000, Olympus, Japan). The cell nuclei are stained with DAPI and emit blue fluorescence upon excitation by a 405 ​nm laser. CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS exhibit green fluorescence when excited by a 488 ​nm laser. Lysosomes stained with Lyso-Tracker Red display red fluorescence upon excitation by a 559 ​nm laser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell CCK-8 experiment of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Cell proliferation and toxicity were analyzed using a CCK-8 kit to assess the cytotoxicity of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles with a fully automatic enzyme label detector [5]. LLC cells were seeded in a 96-well plate (cell density: 1 ​× ​105 ​cells/well) and incubated at 37 ​°C for 24 ​h. The culture medium was removed, and different concentrations of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle solution (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200 ​μg/mL) were added to each well, followed by another 24-h incubation.

Afterward, the culture medium was removed, the cells were washed once with PBS, and 10 ​μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well. The plate was incubated for 2 ​h. The optical density (OD) value of each well was measured using an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay reader (detection wavelength 450 ​nm), and the results were recorded.

Cell survival rate (%) was calculated as [(A s - A b)/(A c - A b)] ​× ​100, where A s is the absorbance of the experimental well (containing cells, culture medium, CCK-8, and the tested compound), A b is the absorbance of the blank well (containing culture medium and CCK-8), and A c is the absorbance of the control well (containing cells, culture medium, and CCK-8).
The Living and Dead Cells Staining assay is performed to demonstrate the cytotoxic effect of nanoparticles on tumor cells using an inverted fluorescence microscope (IX71) instrument [54].

Preparation of 1 ​× ​Assay Buffer: Retrieve the 10 ​× ​Assay Buffer from the low-temperature refrigerator, take an appropriate amount under sterile conditions, and dilute it tenfold with deionized water to obtain the 1 ​× ​Assay Buffer.

Preparation of 1 ​× ​Staining Working Solution: Allow the stored Calcein-AM solution (2 ​mmol ​L−1) and PI solution (1.5 ​mmol ​L−1) to equilibrate to room temperature for 20–30 ​min. Take 5 ​μL of Calcein-AM solution (2 ​mmol ​L−1) and 15 ​μL of PI solution (1.5 ​mmol ​L−1), then add them to 5 ​mL of 1 ​× ​Assay Buffer. Mix thoroughly to obtain the working solution with a final concentration of 2 ​μmol ​L−1 for Calcein-AM and 4.5 ​μmol ​L−1 for PI.

Solutions of the Control group (saline), MTX group (3.04 ​μg/mL), CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA group (200 ​μg/mL), and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles group (200 ​μg/mL) are added to 6-well plates inoculated with LLC cells at an appropriate density (50 ​%–60 ​%), followed by incubation for 24 ​h. The cells are then digested with trypsin-EDTA and collected by centrifugation (1000 r∙min−1, 3 ​min). The supernatant is removed, and the cells are washed three times with 1 ​× ​Assay Buffer to eliminate residual esterase activity. A cell suspension is prepared using 1 ​× ​Assay Buffer with a density of 1 ​× ​105∼1 ​× ​106 ​cells/mL. 100 ​μL of the staining working solution is mixed with 200 ​μL of the cell suspension and incubated at 37 ​°C for 15 ​min. Using a fluorescence microscope with a 490 ​± ​10 ​nm excitation filter, live cells (green fluorescence) and dead cells (red fluorescence) are detected simultaneously. Dead cells are observed exclusively using an emission filter of 545 ​nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo ROS detection (DHE confocal staining)
ce:section.ce:para: ROS detection is performed using the FV1000 confocal microscope to demonstrate that nanoparticles induce tumor cell death through ROS production [54]. A cell slide is placed at the bottom of a 24-well plate, and LLC cells (approximately 2 ​× ​104 ​cells/well) are inoculated. The plate is cultured in an incubator for 24 ​h, after which the culture medium is discarded. The control group (saline), MTX group (3.04 ​μg/mL), CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticle group (200 ​μg/mL), and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle group (200 ​μg/mL) are added to the corresponding culture medium. The plate is then incubated for 6 ​h, and the culture medium is discarded. Subsequently, the cells are washed three times with PBS to remove any residual nanoparticles. A 500 ​μL solution of DHE (5 ​μmol ​L−1) is added, and the plate is incubated at 37 ​°C for 30 ​min. The cells are then washed three times with PBS. Following this, the cells are fixed with 4 ​% paraformaldehyde for 30 ​min and washed three times with PBS. The cell slide is removed and fixed (cell side down) onto a glass slide containing mounting medium (with DAPI) and allowed to dry naturally in the dark. The samples are observed and imaged using the FV1000 confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan). Cell nuclei stained with DAPI emit blue fluorescence upon excitation with a 405 ​nm laser. CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticles exhibit green fluorescence upon excitation with a 488 ​nm laser. DHE staining displays red fluorescence under excitation by a 559 ​nm laser.

Male C57BL/6JNifdc mice, aged 6–8 weeks, are procured from Beijing Weitong Lihua Company. All mice are housed in animal facilities under controlled environmental conditions (room temperature: 22 ​± ​1 ​°C, relative humidity: 40 ​%–70 ​%). All animal experiments are approved by the Ethics Committee of the Second Hospital of Hebei Medical University. Each mouse is subcutaneously injected with 1 ​× ​106 LLC cells into the right anterior chest to establish a murine tumor model.

The C57BL/6JNifdc tumor-bearing mice model is established as described in section 2.8. Once the tumor volume reaches 50 ​mm3, forty tumor-bearing mice are randomly assigned to four groups: a) Control group (200 ​μL of physiological saline); b) MTX group (200 ​μL, 0.0608 ​mg/mL); c) CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticles group (200 ​μL, 4 ​mg/mL); d) CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles group (200 ​μL, 4 ​mg/mL). The administration route for all groups is intravenous injection via the tail vein. On 0, 5, and 10 ​d after grouping, repeated tail vein injections are performed, and all groups are exposed to daylight for 24 ​h. The tumor volume of the tumor-bearing mice is measured every two days. The general condition, tumor volume, and body weight of the mice are recorded every other day. On day 12, 5 mice from each group are randomly euthanized, and tumor, heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney tissues are collected for Hematoxylin-Eosin (HE) staining and TUNEL staining. When the tumor volume increases to 1000 ​mm3, tumor-bearing mice are considered dead, and the date is recorded.

The inverted fluorescence microscope IX71 is used to perform HE staining to demonstrate the antitumor effect of nanoparticles on tumor tissue through the instrument [55]. Fresh tumor tissues from the Control group, MTX group, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticles group, and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles group are immersed in 4 ​% paraformaldehyde solution for 12 ​h. The tissues are then dehydrated using ethanol at different concentrations (75 ​%, 85 ​%, 95 ​%, 100 ​%) and cleared in xylene using a tissue dehydrator. After immersion in wax for 8 ​h, the tumor tissue is embedded in paraffin. The paraffin sections are dewaxed in xylene, followed by a series of ethanol concentrations (100 ​%, 95 ​%, 85 ​%, 75 ​%), and PBS. The sections are stained with hematoxylin for 2 ​min and washed with tap water for 10 ​min. They are then differentiated in a 1 ​% hydrochloric acid-ethanol solution for about 30 ​s and rinsed with tap water. The sections are stained with eosin solution for 8 ​s and rinsed with tap water until colorless. The samples are dehydrated using ethanol at different concentrations (85 ​%, 95 ​%, 100 ​%), cleared in xylene, mounted with neutral gum, and observed under an optical microscope for imaging.

TUNEL staining is employed to demonstrate the cytotoxic effect of nanoparticles on tumor tissue using the FV1000 confocal microscope instrument [56]. The TUNEL detection solution consists of 75 ​μL TdT enzyme, 675 ​μL fluorescent labeling solution, and 750 ​μL TUNEL detection buffer. Tumor tissue sections from the Control group, MTX group, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticles group, and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles group are fixed in 4 ​% paraformaldehyde solution for 30 ​min and washed twice with PBS for 10 ​min each time. Next, 0.5 ​% Triton X-100 in PBS is added, and the sections are incubated at room temperature for 5 ​min. The sections are then washed twice with PBS for 10 ​min each. 100 ​μL of TUNEL detection solution is added to the samples, which are incubated at 37 ​°C for 60 ​min (in a humidification box to prevent evaporation) in the dark. After incubation, the sections are washed three times with PBS for 10 ​min each. Finally, slides are mounted using anti-fade mounting medium and allowed to dry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metabolic distribution of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles in vivo and the detection of serum free lead in mice are designed to clarify the distribution and safety of the nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Small Animal In Vivo Imager IVIS Lumina III and Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (3) are used [57]. A tumor model is established in 28C57BL/6JNifdc mice. Once the tumors reach a volume of 50 ​mm3, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles (200 ​μL, 4 ​mg/mL) are administered via tail vein injection to 7 tumor-bearing mice. Fluorescence intensity of the tumors, feces, and major organs is measured by randomly sacrificing one mouse at each time point (0, 6, 24, 48, 72, 96, and 120 ​h). The fluorescence intensity of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles in tumors, feces, and major organs is assessed using a small animal live imager to show the biodistribution of the nanoparticles. Additionally, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles (200 ​μL, 4 ​mg/mL) are injected into 21 tumor-bearing mice via the tail vein, and blood samples are collected at different time points (0 ​h, 6 ​h, 24 ​h, 48 ​h, 72 ​h, 96 ​h, and 120 ​h). Blood samples are collected from three mice at each time point. Serum free lead concentration is measured by ICP-MS.

A fully automatic biochemical and blood cell analyzer is used in the safety experiment to assess the safety of nanoparticles in mice [58]. Healthy C57BL/6JNifdc mice are selected for the in vivo biosafety analysis. A total of 20 mice are randomly divided into four groups: Control group, MTX group, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticles group, and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX nanoparticles group. All groups are administered via tail vein injection once every 5 days for a total of three injections. One month later, blood samples are randomly collected from three mice in each group by eyeball bleeding for routine blood tests, as well as liver and kidney function tests. Additionally, two mice from each group are sacrificed to collect major organs (including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) for HE staining. The specific sampling and staining procedures are the same as described in Step 2.10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectric mechanism detection of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles mixed solution is spin-coated onto an FTO/SnO2 substrate and dried at 100 ​°C for 30 ​min to obtain a CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles film. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) is then used to detect variations in the film's contact potential difference under dark and illuminated conditions.

LLC cells are inoculated into 6-well plates (approximately 1 ​× ​106 ​cells/well) and cultured in an incubator for 24 ​h. The culture medium is then discarded, and the control group (saline), MTX group (3.04 ​μg/mL), CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticles group (200 ​μg/mL), and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles group (200 ​μg/mL) are added to their respective culture media. After 24 ​h, the cells are washed twice with PBS, digested with trypsin, and centrifuged at 2235 r∙min−1 for 5 ​min to collect them. The collected cells are washed three times with PBS and resuspended in fresh lysis buffer to a density of 1 ​× ​107 ​cells/mL. After standing for 5 ​min, the specimen is centrifuged at 3353 r∙min−1 for 10 ​min at 2–8 ​°C, and the supernatant is collected for detection. A tetrahydrofolate detection kit is then used to measure the THFA concentration in tumor cells from each group.

Glutathione detection is employed to demonstrate that nanoparticles kill tumor cells by reducing the concentration of glutathione, using a flow cytometer [59]. LLC cells are inoculated into 6-well plates (approximately 1 ​× ​106 ​cells/well) and cultured in an incubator for 24 ​h. The culture medium is then discarded, and the control group (saline), MTX group (3.04 ​μg/mL), CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticles group (200 ​μg/mL), and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles group (200 ​μg/mL) are added to their respective culture media. After 24 ​h, the cells are washed twice with PBS, digested with trypsin, and centrifuged at 2235 r∙min−1 for 5 ​min to collect them. The collected cells are washed three times with PBS. Membrane-breaking agent (500 ​μL) is added and incubated for 30 ​min at room temperature in the dark, followed by centrifugation at 1118 r∙min−1 for 5 ​min, discarding the supernatant. Then, 2 ​mL BD Perm/Wash is added, mixed well, and incubated for 10 ​min at room temperature in the dark, followed by centrifugation at 1118 r∙min−1 for 5 ​min, and discarding the supernatant. GSH antibody (1:250) is added, mixed, and incubated for 30 ​min at room temperature, protected from light. Afterward, 2 ​mL BD Perm/Wash is added, and the sample is washed once, followed by centrifugation at 1118 r∙min−1 for 5 ​min and discarding the supernatant. R-PE-conjugated AffiniPure F(ab')2 Fragment Donkey Anti-Mouse IgG (H ​+ ​L) secondary antibody (1 : 50) is added, mixed well, and incubated for 20 ​min at room temperature, protected from light. Finally, 2 ​mL BD Perm/Wash is added, followed by centrifugation at 1118 r∙min−1 for 5 ​min, discarding the supernatant, and mixing the cell suspension for flow cytometric analysis.

The release experiment aims to demonstrate the drug loading efficiency of MTX in CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, using a UV–vis spectrophotometer [60]. To evaluate the release of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles in different pH environments, including acidic (pH 5.0), neutral physiological (pH 7.4), and alkaline physiological (pH 8.4), PBS (pH 5.0, pH 7.4, and pH 8.4) is selected as the in vitro release medium. Initially, 40.00 ​mg of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles is accurately weighed and resuspended in 10 ​mL of PBS (pH 5.0, pH 7.4, or pH 8.4). The suspension is placed in a dialysis bag (MW: 3500), which is then submerged in 300 ​mL of PBS (pH 5.0, pH 7.4, or pH 8.4), maintained at 37 ​°C, and stirred at 120 r∙min−1. Samples of 3 ​mL are taken at predetermined time points (0, 5, 10, 15, 30 ​min, 1 ​h, 1.5 ​h, 2 ​h, 4 ​h, 6 ​h, 8 ​h, 10 ​h, 12 ​h, and 24 ​h). The sample volume is replenished with an equal volume of fresh PBS buffer at the corresponding pH value. The drug concentration in the release medium is measured using a spectrophotometer, and the drug release curve is plotted. Three parallel experiments are conducted to confirm reproducibility.

ESR detection [7] is employed to demonstrate that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles generate ROS upon irradiation with 405 ​nm light. First, prepare a 200 ​μg/mL solution of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles. Take 45 ​μL of the solution (200 ​μg/mL) and add 5 ​μL of the corresponding capture agent. 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone hydrochloride (TEMP) is used as the capture agent for 1O2, while DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide) is used as the capture agent for (O2 − and ·OH). The sample is irradiated with a 405 ​nm LED lamp for 10 ​min, followed by ESR detection.

To ensure the accuracy of the experiment, at least three replicates are performed. All data are expressed as mean ​± ​standard deviation. All statistical analyses are conducted using Origin 2018 and GraphPad Prism 8.0. Statistical significance among multiple groups is determined using one-way ANOVA or two-way ANOVA. The Student's t-test is used to evaluate statistical significance between two groups. Values of ∗P ​< ​0.05, ∗∗P ​< ​0.01, and ∗∗∗P ​< ​0.001 are considered statistically significant.

In summary, the novel concept of using CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles as an integrated therapeutic diagnostic platform is proposed. This platform demonstrates exceptional capabilities to track tumor cells via fluorescence detection, deliver MTX for chemotherapy, and generate ROS under visible light exposure (Fig. 1). After injection of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles and subsequent irradiation with visible light, the platform successfully induces a redox imbalance in tumor cells, enhances lipid peroxidation, and ultimately leads to apoptosis [64]. Importantly, the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles exhibit excellent safety and powerful photoelectrocatalytic chemotherapy abilities. These findings underscore the significance of their development and support their potential application in oncotherapy.

The crystallographic structure of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles is characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD peaks at 12.8°, 21.3°, 27.3°, 28.5°, and 42.4° (Fig. 2 a) correspond to the (120), (113), (223), (214), and (314) lattice planes of Cs4PbBr6, respectively [65]. The presence of Cs4PbBr6 is unlikely to affect the photoelectrical properties of the nanoparticles due to its low content. Additionally, the (211) and (220) crystal planes of CsPbBr3 are observed at 34.3° and 38.9° [65]. Since the Sn2+ radius (0.118 ​nm) [66,67] is smaller than the Pb2+ radius (0.119 ​nm) [68,69] in the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 crystals [70–72], the XRD peaks at 15.0°, 19.9°, and 30.2° correspond to the (101), (121), and (202) crystal planes, respectively [73]. Moreover, peaks at 22.3°, 32.5°, 37.6°, 40.7°, and 45.6° correspond to the (110), (200), (211), (220), and (221) crystal planes [65], respectively. Because Sn2+ has a smaller radius than Pb2+, the residual 0D Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals (abbreviated as NCs), previously dispersed in the solvent (cyclohexane), are converted into 3D CsPbBr3 NCs [74], indicating the successful fabrication of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 NCs [71].

To confirm the successful anchoring of methotrexate-chitosan-folic acid (MTX-CS-FA) onto the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 nanocrystals, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy is performed (Fig. 2b). The presence of the amide bond (N-C=O) peak at 1530 ​cm−1 clearly confirms the formation of an amide bond between the carboxyl groups of MTX and FA and the amino group of CS [75], indicating the successful generation of MTX-CS-FA. The C=C-C=O functional groups in the MTX and FA molecules appear at 1637 ​cm−1 [76,77], while the -OH groups of CS are observed in the spectrum between 3200 and 3600 ​cm−1 [76]. Furthermore, the -CH3 groups originating from residual oleic acid (OA) and oleylamine (OAm) during the fabrication of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 are observed at 2925 ​cm−1 [78]. These characteristic functional groups confirm that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 is successfully encapsulated within the MTX-CS-FA shell.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is employed to investigate the encapsulation of the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 nanocrystals within the MTX-CS-FA shell. TEM images (Fig. 2c) show that the as-prepared CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 nanoparticles have a regular and uniform rectangular morphology, with particle sizes ranging from 5 to 20 ​nm. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis (Fig. 2d) reveals clear lattice fringes with an average interplanar distance of 0.60 ​nm, corresponding to the (101) plane spacing as calculated from the XRD patterns (Fig. 2a). The fast Fourier transform (FFT) image (inset, Fig. 2d) and the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 2e) confirm the crystal structure of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3. The CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, exhibiting particle sizes of 20–40 ​nm, have a spherical shape with a uniform MTX-CS-FA coating, forming a core-shell structure (Fig. 2f and g). The (110) and (200) interplanar spacings in the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles are measured as 0.38 and 0.27 ​nm, respectively, as indicated by FFT and SAED (Fig. 2g and h). These findings suggest that the MTX-CS-FA organic shell protects the composition and crystal structure of the perovskite nanocrystals, consistent with the XRD (Fig. 2a) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) results (Fig. S1, Supporting Information).

The stability of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles in a simulated tumor environment (H2O2) is evaluated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 2i–k and Fig. S2). A characteristic N 1s peak at 400.4 ​± ​0.5 ​eV (Fig. 2i) is observed in both CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA(H2O2) [79–81] samples, but not in the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 sample, confirming the stability and non-degradation of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 when encapsulated in MTX-CS-FA under the oxidizing H2O2 tumor environment (Fig. S3). These findings are consistent with the FTIR results (Fig. 2b). Additionally, the Pb 4f peaks (138.4 ​± ​0.5 and 143.3 ​± ​0.5 ​eV) [82] and Sn2+ valence state (486.1 ​± ​0.5 ​eV) [83] (Fig. 2j and k) show minimal change, indicating that the substitution of Sn for Pb does not significantly affect the intrinsic perovskite structure. This is consistent with the XRD analysis (Fig. 2a) and TEM results (Fig. 2g). Therefore, highly stable CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles with diameters of 20–40 ​nm are successfully synthesized and are suitable for subsequent in vivo therapeutic applications.

To investigate the photoelectrocatalytic mechanism, the photoelectric properties and photoelectrocatalytic performance of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles are analyzed using photoluminescence (PL) and Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) (Fig. 3 a and Fig. S4). The PL intensity increases with prolonged immersion in water, and the PL peaks show a redshift from 522 to 527 ​nm over 0–228 ​d, indicating the exceptional stability of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles in water. For comparison with the fresh state, images of the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles in aqueous solutions are further captured using an in vivo imaging system (IVIS) under 405 ​nm light illumination (Fig. 3b) after 228 ​d of immersion. The water-immersed nanoparticles exhibit higher IVIS intensity (more red), consistent with the PL results, demonstrating that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles are stable in water for up to 228 days. The histograms constructed from the data collected over the 0 to 228-day period indicate the generation of photoexcited holes and electrons, which is consistent with the PL results. To further investigate the photoelectrocatalytic properties of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, KPFM is used to measure the contact potential difference (CPD) in the dark and under 405 ​nm light irradiation (Fig. 3c–f, Fig. S5). The local surface potential, derived from the CPD between the tip and sample surface, depends on their respective work functions [84,85]. The CPD is calculated using the formula CPD = (ϕ _tip - ϕ_sample)/(-e), where ϕ _tip and ϕ _sample represent the work functions of the tip and sample, respectively, and e is the electron charge. The increased generation of photoexcited holes and electrons results in an increase in the CPD [86]. Under dark conditions, the in situ CPD from the three-dimensional topography image is 0.28 ​V (Fig. 3c). Under light conditions (405 ​nm, lx ​= ​491 ​W/m2), the CPD increases to 0.44 ​V, indicating a photoinduced increase of approximately 0.16 ​eV, as shown in Fig. 3f. This photoinduced increase in CPD suggests that illumination facilitates electron-hole pair separation in CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles [87]. The potential increases by 57 ​% under illumination compared to dark conditions, confirming the favorable photoelectrocatalytic characteristics of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles. This increase in potential after illumination can be attributed to the separation of electrons and holes [88], generating an electric potential that can act on tumor cells. The generated electrons produce reactive ROS, while the holes oxidize GSH, thereby inducing tumor cell death [53,89]. These findings validate the mechanism depicted in Fig. 1 and demonstrate the high efficacy of tumor cell killing by CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles.

Photoelectrocatalysis plays a crucial role in tumor treatment using CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, making it essential to delve into their underlying mechanism. These nanoparticles exert antitumor effects through three primary pathways. Tumor cells often exhibit high levels of antioxidant system components to mitigate oxidative stress. GSH, a key element in the cellular antioxidant defense, is typically overexpressed in tumor cells [90]. Previous studies have shown that the depletion of GSH disrupts redox homeostasis, leading to cellular damage and even apoptosis [91]. Upon excitation by visible light, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles generate electrons (e−) and holes (h+) (Fig. 4 a). The overexpressed GSH in tumor cells can interact with the holes, leading to continuous GSH depletion [54,92,93]. To verify this mechanism, four groups were prepared: Control (I), MTX (II) (3.04 ​μg/mL), CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA (III) nanoparticles (200 ​μg/mL), and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA (IV) nanoparticles (200 ​μg/mL), which were then applied to mouse lung cancer cells (LLC cells). Flow cytometry was used to measure intracellular GSH levels after treatment [6,94]. The fluorescence intensities for the Control, MTX CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticle, and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle groups were 92.4 ​%, 87.7 ​%, 85.4 ​%, and 59.2 ​%, respectively. The CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle group exhibited a significant 36% reduction in fluorescence compared to the Control group (Fig. 4b and c), suggesting that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles promote antitumor effects by oxidizing GSH via the generated holes [95,96].

The production of ROS, including superoxide anions (O2 −), ·OH, and 1O2, depends on electron transfer in an oxygen-rich environment. An increase in ROS levels can disrupt protein and DNA structures, ultimately resulting in apoptosis [7,97,98]. To verify that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles can generate ROS, LLC cells were subjected to Dihydroethidium (DHE) confocal staining to detect intracellular ROS after different treatments [99]. The results show that the fluorescence intensities of the Control, MTX, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticle, and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle groups (Fig. 4d and e) were 0, 0, 6.40, and 56.28, respectively. The CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles exhibited significantly higher fluorescence intensity compared to the Control group (P ​< ​0.001), suggesting that these nanoparticles can generate a substantial amount of O2 −. Furthermore, the major types of ROS were quantitatively analyzed using electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy to detect 1O2. Intense characteristic signals for O2 −, ·OH, and 1O2 are observed (Fig. 4f–h), indicating that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles produce O2 −, ·OH, and 1O2 upon exposure to visible light at 405 ​nm. Tetrahydrofolate (THFA) is an essential coenzyme in the synthesis of purine nucleotides and pyrimidine deoxynucleotides. Methotrexate (MTX), as an inhibitor of dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), blocks the conversion of dihydrofolate (DHFA) into THFA [64,100]. This blockade of purine synthesis leads to cell cycle arrest in the S phase [62,63]. Additionally, MTX inhibits DHFR activity, thereby obstructing the GCH1/BH4/DHFR pathway, which promotes lipid peroxidation and triggers apoptosis [62–64]. To investigate the effect of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles on THFA concentration, ELISA assays were conducted on LLC cells after treatment. The THFA concentrations in the Control, MTX, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticle, and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle groups (Fig. 4i) were 1.56, 0.95, 1.02, and 0.29 ​ng/mL, respectively. Moreover, the THFA concentration in the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle group significantly decreased by 81.41 ​% and 42.31 ​% compared to the Control group and MTX group, respectively (P ​< ​0.001), suggesting that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles significantly enhance the antitumor effects of MTX chemotherapy [61,101], leading to lipid peroxidation in tumour cells and apoptosis.

CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles significantly increased the LLC cell death rate to 83 ​% (P ​< ​0.001) (Fig. 5 a and b), confirming that these nanoparticles cause fragmented cell death in LLC cells (Fig. 5a). To evaluate the impact of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles on LLC cell viability, a CCK-8 assay was performed. LLC cells were treated with different concentrations of nanoparticles (0–200 ​μg/mL) [102], and the viability of LLC cells treated with 200 ​μg/mL CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles was significantly reduced to only 37 ​% (Fig. 5c). This indicates a 2.7-fold decrease in cell viability (100 ​% vs. 37 ​%), highlighting a substantial reduction in LLC cell viability at this nanoparticle concentration.

To investigate the MTX loading in CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, UV–Vis spectrophotometry was used, and the drug loading rate was found to be 1.52 ​± ​0.03 ​% (Fig. 5d) [103], confirming the successful incorporation of MTX into the nanoparticles. To evaluate the release of MTX from CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles at different pH values [104], an in vitro release experiment was conducted. After 1.5 ​h, the cumulative MTX release at pH 8.4 was 51.17 ​± ​3.84 ​% (Fig. 5e), suggesting significant MTX release in the weakly alkaline environment typical of tumor cells [61].

To assess the FA-mediated targeting efficacy, confocal microscopy [105] was used to observe the fluorescence intensity in mouse myoblast (C2C12) and LLC cells after incubation with CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA for 6 ​h. The fluorescence intensity in LLC cells was 2.9 times higher than in C2C12 ​cells (Fig. 5f), confirming the significant tumor cell targeting effect of FA [106]. Further confirmation of the targeting effect provided by FA was achieved by measuring the fluorescence intensities of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS and CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA nanoparticles in LLC cells after 6 ​h of incubation. The fluorescence intensity in the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS-FA group was 2.8 times higher than in the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@CS group (Fig. 5f and g), further demonstrating the tumor cell-targeting effect of FA [106,107].

Finally, confocal microscopy was used to detect the fluorescence intensity of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles in LLC cells to demonstrate their cytotoxicity. The results indicated that after 24 ​h of incubation, almost no fluorescence was observed in the lysosomes of LLC cells, indicating lysosomal rupture [108]. This rupture not only facilitates the release of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles from the lysosomes but also triggers the release of MTX, which contributes to tumor cell apoptosis (Fig. 5f and g).
In conclusion, CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles at a concentration of 200 ​μg/mL demonstrate both significant cytotoxicity and precise LLC cell targeting.

To further evaluate the antitumor effect of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, mouse models were established by subcutaneously injecting LLC cells into C57BL/6JNifdc mice. Once the tumor volume reached 50 ​mm3, the tumor-bearing mice were intravenously injected with CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, with a total of three injections administered at five-day intervals. On day 12, the tumor-bearing mice were euthanized (Fig. 6 a).

To demonstrate the in vivo targeting effect of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, the fluorescence intensity of the nanoparticles in tumor-bearing mice was detected using a small animal IVIS system (Fig. 6b and c) [94]. Prior to injection, the fluorescence intensity in the subcutaneous tumor was barely detectable. However, 24 ​h post-injection, the fluorescence intensity increased by 108 ​% compared to pre-injection levels, confirming the precise targeting of these nanoparticles [109].

To assess the antitumor effect on subcutaneous tumors, tumor volume, weight, and survival time (in days) of the tumor-bearing mice were measured every two days (Fig. 6d–f) [110]. On day 12, the average tumor volume in the Control group was 1785.44 ​mm3, while the average tumor volume in the mice injected with CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles was only 396.11 ​mm3, a 4.51-fold difference. Similarly, the average tumor weight in the Control group was 2.31 ​g, whereas in mice injected with CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, the average weight was only 0.52 ​g, a 4.44-fold difference. Additionally, the mice that received CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles via tail vein injection survived for up to 27 days (Figs. S6 and S7). These results confirm the significant inhibitory effects of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles on the growth of LLC subcutaneous tumors in mice.

To assess acute toxicity [56,111], the body weights of the mice in each group were measured (Fig. 6g). There was negligible fluctuation in the body weights of the mice across all groups. On day 12, the average body weight of mice in the Control group was 24.11 ​g, while the average body weight of mice injected with CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles was 22.50 ​g, indicating that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles did not exhibit significant toxicity in mice.

To demonstrate the photocatalytic therapeutic and chemotherapeutic efficacy of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase-mediated dUTP nick-end labeling (TUNEL) staining were performed (Fig. 6h and i) [55]. The H&E staining images revealed a significant reduction in tumor cells in the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle group compared to the Control group. Similarly, the TUNEL staining results showed that apoptotic cells accounted for 3.61 ​% of the total cells in the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle group (P ​< ​0.001) (Fig. 6h).

In summary, the in vivo tumor results confirm that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles exhibit significant photoelectrocatalytic enhancement under visible light illumination, contributing to the targeted inhibition of tumor growth.

To evaluate the biological safety and metabolic distribution of CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles in mice, histopathological assessments, routine blood examinations, liver and kidney function tests, and weight measurements were conducted (Fig. 7 a). The non-inflammatory and non-damaging properties of the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle group were assessed through histological analysis of major mouse organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys [112,113].

Additionally, there were no significant differences in routine blood parameters, liver or kidney function, or body weight between the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticle group and the Control group (Fig. 7b), indicating good in vivo biocompatibility of the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles.

To further assess the safety of these nanoparticles, the level of Pb2+ in the blood was measured using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) to analyze the serum free lead concentration in tumor-bearing mice (Fig. 7c) [5,56]. The results showed that the average free lead concentration in the serum of tumor-bearing mice was 7.60 ​μg/L on day 5, which is within the normal and safe range. While the Control group had a free lead concentration of 3.62 ​μg/L, there was no significant difference between the two groups (P ​> ​0.05). Both concentrations are significantly lower than the reference blood lead level (50 ​μg/L) established by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention [114], suggesting that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles have excellent biological safety in mice.

Subsequently, an IVIS system was used to monitor the fluorescence intensities of the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles in tumor-bearing mouse tissues and feces, to determine their in vivo metabolic distribution (Fig. 7d) [115]. Following injection, the fluorescence intensities in the tumor and liver were 26.6 ​× ​106 and 413 ​× ​106, respectively, within the first 24 ​h, while that in the kidneys at 48 ​h was 28.9 ​× ​106. Remarkably, after 120 ​h, fluorescence intensity in the feces was 81.4 ​× ​106, and fluorescence signals in other organs had disappeared (Fig. 7e). This confirms that the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles selectively target tumor cells, undergo metabolism in the liver and kidneys, and are eventually excreted from the mouse body in the feces.

These findings demonstrate that CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles have excellent biological safety in vivo and are excreted from the body in the form of fluorescent perovskite. The MTX-CS-FA shell effectively prevents the degradation of PeNCs in water and the release of metallic ions into the bloodstream.

In summary, we have successfully conjugated CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3 with the MTX-CS-FA complex, resulting in the formation of highly water-stable CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles. These innovative nanoparticles exhibit exceptional photoelectrocatalytic activity for tumor therapy, which can be attributed to their precise tumor cell targeting, bioimaging capability, and ability to induce cancer cell apoptosis. Upon illumination with visible light, the CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles generate ROS through electron excitation, eliminate overexpressed GSH via oxidation by the generated holes, and inhibit DHFR activity with MTX. These combined mechanisms work synergistically to promote lipid peroxidation, ultimately leading to apoptosis. The CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles demonstrated significant antineoplastic effects in both cellular and in vivo animal models. Furthermore, the nanoparticles exhibit acceptable biological safety in vivo and can be excreted from the mouse body without decomposition, preserving their perovskite quantum dot structure. The CsSn0.5Pb0.5Br3@MTX-CS-FA nanoparticles, with their photoelectrocatalytic and chemotherapeutic properties, have the potential to offer a novel approach for oncotherapy.

J. Zhao, H. Bu and X. Chen designed the study and supervised the project. J. Li synthesized the materials, cell experiments, and in vivo experiments. Y. Zhang carried out the antitumor experiments. R. Yan carried out XRD, FTIR, and TEM experiments. K. Sun carried out data correction. X. Chen and X. Liu carried out data correction and grammar check of the manuscript. Y. Jiao carried out the AFM tests. J. Shi provided important suggestions.
